title: Central Banks Turn Hawkish in Concert as Debasement Trade Unravels
subhead: BOJ summary reveals broad support for continued rate hikes; Fed hike bets surge to 36% for July; ECB's Lane warns inflation stays sticky even post-Iran deal. Gold crashes below $4,000. Oil slides to pre-war lows as Hormuz traffic recovers.

sections.heading: BOJ Summary Signals Sustained Tightening Cycle
sections.body:
The Bank of Japan released the summary of opinions from its June 15-16 meeting on Wednesday, revealing that multiple board members called for continued interest rate increases. One member said it is 'desirable' to consider rate hikes 'at a pace of every few months,' citing a neutral rate around 2%. Another argued the BOJ 'should maintain its stance to continue to raise the policy interest rate.' The board voted 7-1 to hike rates to around 1% from 0.75%, with dissenter Toichiro Asada warning that tightening 'could suppress aggregate demand by curbing firms' business fixed investment.' Governor Kazuo Ueda was absent from the meeting due to hospitalization.

Separately, hawkish board member Naoki Tamura told business leaders in Hyogo on Thursday that underlying inflation had already reached the BOJ's 2% target and that the bank should consider speeding up the pace of tightening if inflation risks intensify, according to the Wall Street Journal. Some economists note that Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi is seen as favoring loose monetary policy, which may constrain the pace of further hikes. Meanwhile, Japanese authorities are planning to improve management of their $1.3 trillion in FX reserves to support potential currency intervention, according to Econotimes, as verbal warnings have failed to arrest the yen's decline toward 40-year lows against the dollar.
sections.whyItMatters: Japan is moving from an era of extraordinary monetary easing toward normalization, with the pace of tightening now the key question. The 7-1 vote and explicit discussion of a 2% neutral rate signal the BOJ is serious about catching up — but political pressure from Takaichi and yen weakness complicate the calculus.

sections.heading: Fed Hike Bets Surge as Hawkish Consensus Builds
sections.body:
Markets are now pricing a 36% probability of a Fed rate hike at the July meeting, up dramatically from 8.5% just one week ago, according to CME FedWatch data cited by Econotimes. For September, the probability of a rate rise has climbed above 70%, up from 29.1%. The shift comes as Fed officials sound increasingly hawkish, with new Fed Chair Kevin Warsh's appointment driving expectations that the central bank will prioritize inflation fighting.

The dollar hit a 13-month high against a basket of currencies as investors rotated into safe-haven assets amid tech stock selloffs and building rate hike expectations. US new home sales fell 7.3% in May to 580K, well below the 638K forecast, while the current account deficit widened to $226.8B in Q1 — signs that monetary tightening is already compressing housing demand even before a second hike. Fed Governor Cook is scheduled to speak later Wednesday, and bank stress test results are due.
sections.whyItMatters: The rapid repricing of Fed expectations — from near-zero to above 70% for a September hike in one week — is itself the story. It reflects a shift in market perception of Warsh's Fed and suggests the 'higher for longer' narrative is becoming 'higher, sooner.'

sections.heading: Gold Crashes Below $4,000 as Debasement Trade Unwinds
sections.body:
Spot gold fell below $4,000 per ounce on Wednesday for the first time since November 2025, marking a 28% decline from its January peak of $5,600. Silver has plunged more than 50% from its record near $120, now trading below $59. The sell-off is driven by the same force reshaping global monetary policy: expectations that major central banks will tighten, not ease, amid persistent inflation.

According to CoinDesk, markets are now pricing in two 25 basis point rate hikes by March 2027, which would lift the federal funds rate to 4.00%-4.25%. JPMorgan noted that gold's increased share of central bank reserves is largely a mark-to-market effect rather than active accumulation, according to SeekingAlpha. Bitcoin has also fallen below $62,000, a 50% correction from its October all-time high, now trading below its 200-week moving average — though BTC has gained roughly 30% against gold and 55% against silver since February.
sections.whyItMatters: The 2025 'debasement trade' thesis — that fiscal deficits would erode fiat and lift gold, silver, and BTC together — is breaking down. Central banks choosing to fight inflation rather than accommodate it undermines the core logic that drove gold to $5,600.

sections.heading: Oil Slides to Pre-War Lows as Hormuz Traffic Recovers
sections.body:
Brent crude fell more than $3 on Wednesday, with prices dropping to their lowest levels since before the Iran war began. WTI also fell below key technical levels. The decline reflects growing confidence that oil will flow more freely through the Strait of Hormuz following the US-Iran ceasefire and peace deal.

Maritime tracking firm Kpler recorded 25 commodity ship transits through Hormuz on Tuesday and 17 on Wednesday, with figures likely to rise as more crossings are identified. The UN's International Maritime Organization launched a phased evacuation programme this week using temporary northern and southern shipping corridors to move hundreds of stranded vessels and roughly 11,000 seafarers.

However, Iran's Revolutionary Guards Corps (IRGC) rejected the IMO's evacuation routes on Thursday, calling any route announced without Tehran's coordination 'unacceptable and dangerous.' The IRGC warned that ships failing to comply with Iranian transit instructions would face action and said safe transit is possible only via routes designated by Iran, requiring coordination on VHF Channel 16. President Trump said the strait would be 'fully opened' when the deal is signed in Switzerland on Friday.
sections.whyItMatters: Oil is pricing in a return to normal — but the IRGC's rejection of the UN evacuation routes and insistence on controlling Hormuz transit shows that 'normal' will be on Tehran's terms. The gap between market optimism and physical reality on the water is worth watching.

sections.heading: ECB Warns Inflation Stays Sticky Despite Iran Deal
sections.body:
ECB Chief Economist Philip Lane said the central bank will remain 'proactive' in its fight against high inflation even after the US-Iran deal brought down energy prices, according to Reuters. Lane warned that euro zone inflation could stay above the ECB's 2% target for some time, describing the current situation as a 'mid-sized inflation shock' that requires a measured response, in comments reported by MSN on June 19.

ECB Governing Council member Pierre Wunsch kept the door open for a July rate hike, telling Yahoo Finance that the ECB may raise rates one more time if it sees evidence of persistent inflation. The ECB raised its deposit rate by 25 basis points earlier this month — its first hike in three years — as Middle East energy shocks reshaped European monetary policy, according to CryptoBriefing. The German Ifo Business Climate Index came in at 85.6 for June, matching expectations, with Current Assessment at 87.0 (above the 86.0 forecast) and Expectations at 84.1 (below the 85.0 forecast).
sections.whyItMatters: The ECB is threading a narrow path: acknowledging that the Iran deal eases one inflation driver while insisting that core pressures remain. Lane's 'mid-sized shock' framing and Wunsch's July hike signal suggest the ECB is serious about a second move, even as growth indicators soften.

sections.heading: G7 Sanctions Escalation: Shadow Fleet Seizures, Critical Minerals, and DeepSeek Delay
sections.body:
The UK seized a Russian shadow fleet tanker, the Smyrtos, in the English Channel on Sunday — the first such seizure by British forces. Royal Marine Commandos boarded the vessel in a six-hour operation. The ship's captain, Indian national Ajay Pant (38), was charged with contravening Russia sanctions. The UK then added 70 new sanctions targeting Russia's shadow fleet, military procurement networks, and illicit finance, including a front company called Neptune that procures western technology for Russian military intelligence (GRU). The total number of sanctioned shadow fleet and Russian LNG vessels now exceeds 600. Canada separately imposed 162 new sanctions targeting Russia's war machine, announced by Prime Minister Mark Carney during a meeting with Zelenskyy at the G7 summit, covering individuals, entities, and vessels involved in shadow fleet operations, energy revenues, and defense-industrial supply chains.

At the same G7 summit, PM Takaichi proposed a framework for joint stockpiling of critical minerals among G7 nations, with leaders agreeing to reduce dependence on any single non-G7 supplier for rare earths and permanent magnets to below 60% by 2030. China has tightened rare earth export controls since April 2025, and this week detained two Japanese nationals in Dalian on suspicion of smuggling rare earth products — a signal that Beijing's controls are expanding from customs delays to criminal enforcement. Separately, the Trump administration has delayed blacklisting DeepSeek, CXMT, and more than 100 Chinese companies flagged as security risks for nearly eight months, prioritizing the China trade truce over export controls, according to multiple reports.
sections.whyItMatters: The G7 is widening its toolkit — from physically seizing shadow fleet vessels (a first) to building alternative supply chains for critical minerals. But the DeepSeek delay shows the limits of coalition discipline: the US is trading export control urgency for trade diplomacy, while China is escalating from export delays to detention of foreign nationals.

bottomLine: Wednesday marked a decisive shift in the global monetary policy landscape: the BOJ's summary revealed broad board support for continued rate hikes toward a 2% neutral rate, Fed hike probabilities for July surged from 8.5% to 36% in a single week, and ECB officials kept a July move on the table. The synchronized hawkish turn is dismantling the 2025 'debasement trade' that drove gold to $5,600 — spot gold is now below $4,000 for the first time since November. Oil fell sharply as Hormuz transit volumes recover, but Iran's rejection of UN evacuation routes introduces friction between market pricing and physical logistics. On the sanctions front, the G7 is escalating from financial penalties to physical interdiction of Russian shadow fleet vessels, while simultaneously laying the groundwork for critical mineral supply chain decoupling from China — though Washington's delay in blacklisting DeepSeek and 100+ Chinese firms reveals the tension between strategic decoupling and trade diplomacy.
sources: Jiji Press/ADNKronos (BOJ summary), Wall Street Journal (Tamura hawkish comments), Econotimes (FX/market data, Ifo, oil), CME FedWatch via Econotimes (hike probabilities), CoinDesk (gold/debasement trade), SeekingAlpha (JPMorgan on central bank reserves), Reuters/Irish Times (UK shadow fleet sanctions, Carney/Canada), The Deep Dive (Canada 162 sanctions detail), Economic Times India (Iran/IRGC Hormuz routes), Kpler (Hormuz transit data), UPI (China rare earth detentions/Japan), Bloomberg (China throttling mineral exports to Japan), Japan Times/ADNKronos (Takaichi G7 minerals proposal), MSN/CryptoBriefing (ECB Lane, Wunsch, rate hike), Yahoo Finance (DeepSeek blacklist delay)
